title: Compounds (Samāsa) Quiz
description: A quiz on Sanskrit compounds — the dvandva, tatpuruṣa, karmadhāraya, dvigu, bahuvrīhi, and avyayībhāva types — and how a dictionary lists compounds. A cross-dictionary reading skill: most long Sanskrit words you look up are compounds.

source.author: Charles Wikner
source.note: Compound types and examples are grounded in Wikner Lesson 11.B; the dictionary-lookup angle follows the MW reading guide (compounds are nested as hyphen sub-entries).

questions.question: What is a samāsa, and why does it matter for dictionary lookup?
questions.answer: A samāsa is a compound — two or more words joined into a single word. It matters because most long words you meet are compounds; to look one up you usually split it (and dictionaries list compounds under their first member).

questions.question: rāmaḥ ca kṛṣṇaḥ ca → rāmakṛṣṇau ("Rāma and Kṛṣṇa"). What type of compound?
questions.explanation: A dvandva joins members that would otherwise be linked by 'and' (ca). Here the members are taken separately (itaretara), so the compound is dual.

questions.question: sukham ca duḥkham ca → sukhaduḥkham, neuter singular ("pleasure and pain"). Which dvandva subtype?
questions.explanation: A samāhāra dvandva takes the members collectively as a unit — always neuter singular; pairs of opposites often take this form. (An itaretara dvandva, by contrast, is dual/plural.)

questions.question: vṛkṣa-mūlam = vṛkṣasya mūlam ("root of a tree, tree-root"). What type?
questions.explanation: In a tatpuruṣa the first member has a case relationship to the second. Here it's a genitive relation (vṛkṣasya), so a ṣaṣṭhī-tatpuruṣa.

questions.question: A tatpuruṣa can be classified by the case relation of its first member. What is vṛkṣamūlam ("tree-root") called, and why?
questions.answer: A ṣaṣṭhī-tatpuruṣa — because dissolved it is vṛkṣasya mūlam, a genitive (ṣaṣṭhī, 6th-case) relation of the first member to the second.

questions.question: pūrṇa-candraḥ = pūrṇaḥ candraḥ ("full moon") — the members refer to the same object. What subtype?
questions.explanation: A karmadhāraya (samānādhikaraṇa-tatpuruṣa) is descriptive: dissolved, both members have the SAME case (refer to one object) — adjective + noun.

questions.question: A descriptive compound (like a karmadhāraya) but whose first member is a numeral or a direction word — e.g. eka-vacana. What is it called?
questions.explanation: A dvigu has the karmadhāraya sense but a numeral/direction first member (eka- 'one', dvi- 'two', bahu- 'many').

questions.question: a-jñānam = a- + jñānam ("ignorance") — a negative particle as first member. What subtype?
questions.explanation: A nañ-tatpuruṣa has a negative/privative particle (na-, an-, a-) as its first member, giving a negative sense.

questions.question: kumbha-kāra ("potter") = kumbha ("pot") + √kṛ ("to make") — the second member is a root-derivative. What subtype?
questions.explanation: An upapada compound has a dhātu (root) derivative as its second member (here -kāra from √kṛ).

questions.question: padmākṣa describes a person, meaning "whose eyes are like lotuses, lotus-eyed" (padma + akṣa). What type?
questions.explanation: A bahuvrīhi is exocentric: it forms an adjective describing something OUTSIDE the compound (the lotus-eyed person), not the eyes themselves.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a tatpuruṣa and a bahuvrīhi with the same members (e.g. 'lotus-eye')?
questions.answer: A tatpuruṣa names the thing itself (padma-akṣa = 'a lotus-eye'); a bahuvrīhi describes a third thing that POSSESSES it (padmākṣa = 'the one whose eyes are lotuses, lotus-eyed') — it agrees with a noun expressed or understood.

questions.question: sa-krodham ("with anger, angrily") is indeclinable and works as an adverb (sa- + krodha). What type?
questions.explanation: An avyayībhāva is indeclinable (avyaya) and functions as an adverb; first member an indeclinable/prefix, whole in neuter-singular form (cf. yathā-śraddham 'according to one's faith').

questions.question: Name the four main classes of Sanskrit compound.
questions.answer: dvandva (copulative), tatpuruṣa (determinative — with karmadhāraya/dvigu/etc. as subtypes), bahuvrīhi (possessive/exocentric), and avyayībhāva (indeclinable/adverbial).

questions.question: Where does a dictionary like Monier-Williams list a compound such as buddha-kalpa?
questions.answer: As a hyphen-led sub-entry inside the paragraph of its first member (here under Buddha) — the third level of MW's alphabetical order. So to find a compound, look under its first word; the leading hyphen also means that element is separately findable.

questions.question: Why is recognising the compound type useful when you can't find a long word in the dictionary?
questions.answer: It tells you where to split. A tatpuruṣa/karmadhāraya splits between a modifier and head (look up both); a dvandva splits into coordinate members; a bahuvrīhi splits like a tatpuruṣa but is read 'possessing X'. Splitting correctly (undoing sandhi at the seam) lets you look up each member.
